ABSTRACT

Age-related skeletal muscle deterioration, referred to as sarcopenia, poses significant 26 risks  to  astronaut  health  and  mission  success  during  spaceflight,  yet  its  multisystem 27 drivers  remain  poorly  understood.  While  terrestrial  sarcopenia  manifests  gradually 28 through  aging,  spaceflight  induces  analogous  musculoskeletal  decline  within  weeks, 29 providing  an  accelerated  model  to  study  conserved  atrophy  mechanisms.  Here,  we 30 introduced  an  integrative  framework  combining  cross-species  genetic  analysis  with 31 physiological modeling to understand mechanistic pathways in space-induced 32 sarcopenia.  By analyzing rodent and  human  datasets, we  identified  conserved 33 molecular pathways underlying microgravity-induced muscle atrophy, revealing shared 34 regulators  of  neuromuscular  signaling  including  pathways  related  to  neurotransmitter 35 release and regulation, mitochondrial function, and synaptic integration. Building upon 36 these  molecular  insights,  we  developed  a  physiologically  grounded  central  pattern 37 generator model that reproduced spaceflight-induced locomotion deficits in mice. This 38 multi-scale  approach  established  mechanistic  connections  between  transcriptional 39 changes and impaired movement kinetics while identifying potential therapeutic targets 40 applicable to both spaceflight and terrestrial aging-related muscle loss. 41

INTRODUCTION 47

Space exploration is rapidly developing with the increasing number of missions to the 48 International Space Station and beyond. As humans push towards longer spaceflights 49 and journeys to new extraterrestrial planets, understanding the physiological changes 50 due to space travel has become critical. While such concerns are actively studied by 51 researchers, our understanding of the biological burden of spaceflight is largely limited. 52 Moreover, this limitation is further exacerbated by the effects of prolonged exposure to 53 the  microgravity  environment 1 and  cosmic  radiation, 2,3 which  has  been  proven  to  be 54 detrimental to human health. 4 55

56

A well-documented consequence of space exploration is the deterioration of 57 musculoskeletal health. Astronauts who return from space experience muscle atrophy, 58 impaired  muscle  performance,  and loss of strength. 5,6 This process of  muscle 59 degradation resembles sarcopenia (SA), a progressive age-related condition that often 60 emerges  over  several  decades  and  leads  to  muscle  loss  and  function. 7 While  this 61 condition progresses over an individual's lifetime, the development of SA-like pathology 62 is  accelerated  under  microgravity  conditions. 8 Despite  noted  observations  of  muscle 63 and  strength  breakdown, 9 the  exact  mechanisms  and  pathways  by  which  space64 induced SA progresses are poorly understood. 65

66

There  are  major  challenges  to  understanding  how  conditions  in  space  affect  human 67 health.  First,  there  are  limited  experimental  samples  and  astronauts  that  have  been 68 studied on the International Space Station, often due to space constraints, costs, and 69

accessibility. 10 This is important because studying physiological changes in space-like 70 conditions on Earth does not effectively translate to the harsh conditions in microgravity. 71 Additionally, astronauts on average spend on average 6 months per space mission tour, 72 and  thus  there  is  limited  information  on  the  longer-term  effects  of  space  travel  and 73 sample size to study such effects. 11 To address this challenge, previous studies aimed 74 to improve our understanding of spaceflight and SA through the use of in vitro 12 and in 75 vivo rodent models. 13 Despite these efforts, these approaches did not account for the 76 heterogeneity of the human population and the biological differences across mammalian 77 species. As a result, our understanding of a mechanistic pathway to SA conditions is 78 largely unknown. 79

80

To  circumvent  this  limitation,  we  leveraged  a  framework  known  as  Translatable 81 Components  Regression  (TransComp-R),  a  computational  model  that  synthesizes 82 multi-omics  data  from  preclinical  rodent  models  and  human  data  to  predict  disease 83 outcomes in humans. 14,15 The TransComp-R pipeline has been successfully applied to 84 overcome other biomedical challenges including Alzheimer's disease, 16-20 85 osteoarthritis, 1 and  inflammatory  bowel  disease. 14,21 We  couple  this  approach  with  a 86 biophysically grounded model  of central pattern generator (CPG)  networks, 22,23 87 therefore enabling multiscale interrogation of SA. 88

89

90

91

92

In this study, we show that transcriptomics mouse models under spaceflight conditions could  predict  human  outcomes  of  SA.  We  performed  cross-species  computational modeling  to  synthesize  spaceflight  mouse  and  SA  human  data  to  identify  potential

biological  pathways  that  are  involved  in  muscle  degeneration.  To  link  the  observed space-related  SA,  we  built  a  network  of  leaky  integrate-and-fire  neurons  to  build  a computational CPG model that recapitulated locomotion kinetics in healthy conditions. Importantly, incorporation of the predictive transcriptomic data into the model generated similar phenotypic gait responses observed under SA conditions. Thus, this approach enables an improved understanding of mechanisms associated with space-induced SA and may potentially inform therapeutic avenues for SA and other muscle-deteriorating conditions related to spaceflight and on Earth.

We accessed RNA-sequencing spaceflight mouse data from the NASA Open Science Data Repository (OSDR). The publicly available spaceflight dataset (OSD-103) contained  16-week-old  C57BL/6J male mice with a sample size of 6 ground controls and 6 spaceflight conditions. 24,25 The spaceflight group was housed at the International Space  Station  for  37  days  before  euthanasia  and  collection  of  quadricep  muscle samples.  The  ground  control  group  was  housed  on  Earth  for  reference.  We  then acquired  RNA-sequencing  data  of  patients  with  SA  from  Gene  Expression  Omnibus (GEO).  We  selected  three  separate  cohort  studies  that  studied  SA  among  different racial demographics, including Caucasian (GSE111006), Afro-Caribbean (GSE111010), and Chinese (GSE110016) descent. 26,27 Our integration of multiple cohorts allowed us to reference our findings across different human backgrounds.

116

We  applied  the  TransComp-R  model  to  identify  PCs  that  encoded  transcriptomics 117 variation of spaceflight mice that predicted SA outcomes in humans. The TransComp-R 118 pipeline works by computing a mouse principal component analysis (PCA) space and 119 projecting human data into space. Mouse principal components that encode 120 transcriptomics  capable  of  stratifying  human  disease  from  control  groups  by  a  linear 121 regression  can  be  biologically  interpreted  through  pathway  enrichment  analyses  and 122 follow-up studies. 123

124

With  the  TransComp-R  model,  we  first  matched  homologous  gene  pairs  across  the 125 mouse and all three human datasets ( Figure 1a ). We next applied principal component 126 analysis  (PCA)  on  the  mouse  dataset  to  identify  principal  components  (PCs)  that 127 explained  80%  of  the  cumulative  variation  and  projected  the  human  dataset  with  the 128 Chinese cohort with SA and control conditions into the mouse PC space. 129

After  matching  for  homologous  pairs,  we  identified  12,484  genes  shared  across  the mouse and human datasets. We constructed a mouse PCA space and projected the Chinese cohort and identified  7 PCs that  explained  80%  of  the  variance  (83.4%  var. exp). We next calculated how much variance the mouse PCs explained in the human data  to  understand  the  potential  of  translation  from  mouse  to  human  ( Figure  2a ). Among the seven PCs, we found PC1, PC3, and PC6 explained a higher ratio of human variance compared to mouse variance. This finding may suggest that PC1, PC3, and PC6 may have a more pronounced ability to capture variance across species than other PCs. PC3 (10.9% Var Exp) PC6 (2.99% Var Exp) 1500 PC4 (1.65% Var Exp)

Of the seven PCs, we identified PC2, PC3, PC4, and PC7 with our LASSO model to be 165 relevant  eigenvectors  discerning  human  SA  and  control  patients  ( Figure  2b ).  We 166 created a generalized linear regression model incorporating these four PCs and found 167 PC3,  PC4,  and  PC6  to  be  significantly  separating  the  SA  and  control  groups  (p  = 168 0.0286, 0.0371, and 0.0253, respectively). We next visualized the three significant PCs 169 and found that the transcriptomics variation encoded on each PC separated the human 170 SA and control groups ( Figure 2c ). 171

Having identified significant PCs that could distinguish between human SA conditions, 175 we sought to interpret the biological implications using GSEA. Our enrichment analysis 176 showed  genes  contributing to pathways related to neuroactive ligand receptor 177 interactions, immune pathways, and cellular structures were enriched for SA on mouse 178 PC3  ( Figure  3a ).  Genes  that  were  involved  with  neurodegenerative  diseases  and 179 metabolic  pathways  were  associated  with  healthy  groups.  This  indicates  that  these 180 genes  are  regulated  under  healthy  conditions  and  may  become  disrupted  under  SA 181 conditions. 182

On the mouse spaceflight PC4, we found pathways involved with immune signaling and cellular structure to  be  enriched in  the SA  group  ( Figure 3b ).  Furthermore,  we  found neurodegenerative diseases to be involved with control groups, similar to those found in PC3.  Among  the  pathways  enriched  on  PC6,  we  found  neuroactive  ligand  receptor interaction  and  PPAR  signaling  associated  with  SA,  whereas  regulation  of  actin cytoskeleton, adherens junction, and leukocyte transendothelial migration was enriched in control ( Figure 3c ).

We also performed GSEA using the hallmark curation and found signaling mechanisms 199 involved with the immune system to be involved with SA ( Supplementary Figure 1 ). 200 Additionally,  we  found  oxidative  phosphorylation  to  be  consistently  enriched  for  the 201 control group in PC3, PC4, and PC6. Such results further support our findings with the 202 reported KEGG pathway analysis. These findings also show that genes contributing to 203 the  immune  responses,  as  well  as  mitochondrial  function  associated  with  oxidative 204 phosphorylation may  play important roles in the muscle degenerative process 205 associated with SA in humans. 206

Caucasian and Afro-Caribbean demographics

After identifying biological pathways associated with SA and control, we questioned if 210 our findings were relevant in other human demographics since our TransComp-R model 211 synthesized a human Chinese cohort with spaceflight gene expression data. Here, we 212 combined the Caucasian and Afro-Caribbean SA datasets to determine if findings from 213 these populations were also reflected by our findings from the Chinese cohort. 214

215

Different  from the Chinese  cohort, the  Caucasian  and  Afro-Caribbean  datasets 216 contained further annotations on muscle weakness in the subjects. We first excluded 217 any patients from the control groups who had any reported history of muscle weakness 218 or muscle loss. We then combined the dataset and verified that batch effects were not 219 present with PCA ( Supplementary Figure 2 ). We  next used the combined 220 Caucasian/Afro-Caribbean data for a partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS221

We constructed three PLS-DA models for each significant mouse PC that we identified to be significant from our TranComp-R model. We selected a panel of genes driving the separation among the PCs by selecting the top 10 and bottom 10 genes ranked by their scores.  We  then  performed  PLS-DA  and  determined  what  genes  were  strongly contributing  to  the  separation.  Genes  strongly  contributing  to  the  model  contained  a calculated variable importance of projection (VIP) score greater than 1.

We demonstrated a separation of control and SA with the combined human dataset and identified genes that contributed to the separation above average ( Figure 4a ). Within PC3, the human samples were separated among the first latent variable (LV). On this LV,  we  found  genes  that  are  strongly  associated  with  SA,  which  included DLG3 , CALB2 , LHFPL6 ,  and ADAM33 ( Figure  4b ).  The DLG3 and CALB2 genes  are associated with neuron signaling, whereas LHFPL6 and ADAM33 are involved with cellto-cell signaling and communication. Oppositely, genes such as PPP1R12B and CNTF are  associated with  the  control  groups.  These  genes  are  involved  in  helping  muscle contraction  through  myosin  phosphatase  activity  and  enhancing  survival  factors  for neuronal cells, respectively.

Multivariate analysis reveals rodent gait deficits related to orbital spaceflight 266

Space-induced muscle atrophy represents a compelling  model for investigating 267 fundamental  mechanisms  of  sarcopenia.  We  demonstrated  with  pathway  enrichment 268 analysis that neuroactive ligand receptor interaction and PPAR  signaling were 269 associated with SA in both human and rodent models, suggesting conserved genetic 270 determinants for SA. 31 To further augment the relationship between SA changes at the 271 molecular  level,  we  further  presented  a  multivariate  analysis  of  locomotory  deficits  in 272 rodents  following  prolonged  microgravity  exposure  aboard  the  International  Space 273 Station. 274

275

We accessed the spaceflight behavioral assay available on the NASA database (OSD276 478)  which  contains  a  prospective  collection  of  gait  data  between  mice  undergoing 277 spaceflight  in  orbit  aboard  the  ISS  and  ground  control  over  35  days. 32 Pre-flight  and 278

To investigate behavioral contributions, we conducted a loading analysis of tracked gait 295 properties  during  locomotion  and  identified  key  gait  parameters  contribution  to  PC 296 variance  in  our  data  ( Figure 5d ).  For  example,  among  the  first  two  components,  we 297 identified propulsion parameters and stride as primary contributors to observed variance 298 ( Figure 5d ). We found similar results when conducting systematic testing across all gait 299 parameters  across  mice  ( Supplementary  Figure  3 ).  To  further  quantify  this,  we 300 selected the most contributing gait parameters across the first 4 PC components and 301

post-flight gait measurements were conducted across a total of 40 mice (20 mice per group), measuring multiple aspects of locomotion and gait kinematics. We conducted an unsupervised analysis of effects on gait dynamics across spaceflight and ground control by  projecting  the  data  into  a  mouse  PC  subspace  to  extract  behavior  dynamics  that were most affected during spaceflight.

We used PCA of gait dynamics to reveal distinct behavioral signatures  between  preand post-flight conditions ( Figure 5a, b ). We clustered the treatment groups based on chi-squared  confidence  ellipse  and  noted  a  distinct  separation  in  PC  space  that  was more  pronounced  in  spaceflight  groups  compared  to  ground  controls,  suggesting specific microgravity-induced adaptations across mice as opposed to temporal artifacts. The first PC captured 25.5% of total variance followed by 14.8% and 11.3% for PC 2 and  3,  respectively.  Approximately  80%  of  locomotory  variation  is  explained  by  just eight  components  ( Figure  5c ),  suggesting  strongly  shared  effects  across  all  mice undergoing spaceflight.

performed inferential  statistics  across  mice. We  found  significant  alterations  in  propel 302 time (p=0.04), stance time (p=0.007), stride length (p<0.001), and swing time (p<0.001) 303 following  orbital  habitation  ( Figure  5e ).  These  deficits  suggest  impairments  in  both 304 stride generation and balance feedback  mechanisms essential for coordinated 305 movement. 33 Similar  observations  were  noted  in  humans  undergoing  functional  gait 306 tests. 34 Moreover, this analysis demonstrated clear locomotory deficits in mice following 307 spaceflight exposure. Notably, the propelling force and stride kinematics were strongly 308 altered  compared  to  ground  control.  These  coordinated  changes  could  suggest 309 disruption  in  multiple  neural  pathways  governing  both  balance  maintenance  and  gait 310 kinetics, consistent with functional adaptations to microgravity conditions. 34,35 311

Physiological modeling of gait characteristics

To  understand  the  physiological  mechanisms  underlying  space-induced  locomotory deficits as well as build a correlation between key molecular determinants of SA, we first established  a  biologically  plausible  physiological  model  that  captured  the  essential dynamics of spinal locomotor circuits under normal terrestrial conditions. This computational  framework  serves  as  a  substrate  to  later  assess  microgravity-induced alterations, demonstrating how changes in network activity directly translate to muscle function and ultimately to observable gait parameters.

Our  network  model  consisted  of  leaky  integrate-and-fire  neurons  following wellestablished membrane potential and gating dynamics. 22,23,36-38 Importantly,  to  capture oscillatory  dynamics  often  found  during  locomotion,  we  assigned  distinct  flexor  and extensor neural populations that were inhibitory connected to one another. This enabled a  subset  of  neurons  to  generate  patterned  activity  in  the  absence  of  input  to  both regions thus reflecting CPG dynamics ( Figure 6a ). 36 Simulation of the population model drove characteristic oscillatory patterns in membrane potential dynamics between flexor and extensor circuits under normal gravity conditions ( Figure 6b ), with flexors exhibiting more  rapid  depolarization  consistent  with  their  role  in  initiating  limb  movement.  The computational  network  successfully  reproduced  rhythmic  activity  reflecting  central pattern  generators,  with  flexor  neurons  (red)  showing  synchronized  burst  patterns distinct from extensor neurons (blue) during simulated normal locomotion ( Figure 6c ). We used the cumulative output of each group to infer muscle activation patterns which were  used  to  extract  gait  dynamics  ( Figure  6d ).  Gait  kinematics  exhibited  a  strong phase-coherent  response  (67%)  between  extensor  and  flexor  periods  ( Figure  6e ) 346 reflecting coordination between muscle groups. 347

348

Thus, this computational approach creates a powerful platform connecting three levels 349 of  analysis:  neuronal  firing  patterns,  muscle  activation  dynamics,  and  resultant  gait 350 kinetics  ( Figure 6f-h ).  The  normal  phase  relationships,  stride  distributions,  and swing 351 patterns established here provide quantifiable baselines against which space-induced 352 neural  adaptations  can  be  measured  in  subsequent  experiments.  By  bridging cellular 353 neurophysiology to whole-organism locomotion, this model enables mechanistic 354 investigations into how microgravity disrupts motor control at multiple biological levels. 355

To  bridge the gap  between  identified  genetic  determinants  found  by  the  gene enrichment analysis and the physiology of gait characteristics, we modified our network model to exhibit SA phenotypic behavior. This was primarily informed by the identified ligand-gated  genetic  disruption  (G RIN2B and CNR1 ),  during  spaceflight  in  both  mice and human cohorts ( Figure 7a ). We modified the synaptic transmission probability and conductances  while  biasing  the  total  input  variability  into  the  network  of  individual neurons in both flexor and extensor populations.  We noted that, upon this disruption, an  observed  reduction  in  phase-coherence  between  flexor  and  extensor  dynamics compared to  the  healthy  terrestrial  network.  (CoherenceHealthy  =  55%,  CoherenceSA  = 33%, Figure 7b ). The change in the network activity also drove a significant increase in both inferred gait swing time and stride length (p<0.0001, Figure 7c, d ), most likely due to  increase  variability  in  the  CPG  network.  Thus,  these  results  recapitulate  the  same gait alterations observed in cohorts of mice that spend a significant time in space flight ( Figure 5e ).

DISCUSSION

Space-induced SA  is  a  major  physiological  change  that  affects  astronauts.  We  used 406 transcriptomics muscle biopsies of mice exposed to microgravity and human 407 populations with human SA data to understand the effects of space travel on humans. 408 In  this  work,  we  presented  a  comprehensive  framework  to  understand  the  biological 409 pathway and its effect on locomotion phenotypes in space-induced SA. By leveraging 410 TransComp-R, we used a mouse-to-human modeling approach to identify key biological 411 pathways related to neurotransmitter release and regulation, synaptic integration, and 412 mitochondrial  dynamics  associated  with  SA  conditions.  Likewise,  a  comprehensive 413 analysis of locomotion behavior in mice exposed to spaceflight showed strong alteration 414 in gait parameters which we  mechanistically linked  to  a  CPG  network  model 415 incorporating altered identified molecular pathways associated with SA conditions. Our 416 results  established  a  framework  to  link  genetic  determinants  of  SA  to  phenotypically 417 resulting locomotion behavior. 418

419

We identified several immune system pathways altered under conditions of SA, which 420 has  also  been  observed  under  spaceflight  conditions. 39-41 A  study  reported  immune 421 dysregulation and the redevelopment of herpes virus among astronauts on a 6-month 422 orbital space mission. 1 A separate 6-month spaceflight that studied two crew members 423 identified elevated proinflammatory biomarkers in muscle biopsy post-landing. 42 During 424

spaceflight,  blood-derived  cytokines  including  interleukins  and  tumor  necrosis  factor425 alpha  were  also  significantly  elevated  during  flight. 43 Further  investigation  of  such 426 immune pathways may improve our understanding of spaceflight-induced SA. 427

428

Metabolic pathways are actively involved in muscle health as demonstrated by protein 429 alterations during spaceflight. 44 Stein and colleagues reported that whole-body protein 430 synthesis  was  reduced  by  45%  about  preflight  measurements. 45 In  the  same  study, 431 post-flight  measurements  returned  to  91%  of  the  pre-flight  levels,  indicating  a  lack  of 432 complete  recovery  from  space  travel. 45 Additionally,  we  identified  genes  that  were 433 strongly associated with SA through neuromuscular signaling. Neuromuscular junction 434 aging has been proposed to be involved in sarcopenia pathogenesis. 46 The age-related 435 denervation at the neuromuscular junction occurs before muscle atrophy 47 and may be 436 involved in SA pathogenesis. 48 437

438

The biomechanical alterations  we  noted  in  our  study  from  the  behavioral  assay  align 439 with  previously  documented  changes  in  movement  atrophy. 49,50 The  propulsion  and 440 stride  deficits  likely  reflect  preferential  atrophy  of  fast-twitch  extensor  muscles  that 441 generate  locomotive  force,  mirroring  the  fiber-type  selectivity  observed  in  terrestrial 442 sarcopenia  but  compressed  into  a  remarkably  accelerated  timeframe  (~35  days). 35,51 443 The  concurrent  changes  in  stride  parameters  suggest  simultaneous  adaptation  of 444 central  pattern  generators  and  sensorimotor  integration  circuits.  It  is  of  interest  that 445 several key protein pathways were also affected in both humans and mice which could 446 explain some of the downstream locomotion phenotypes. Although our study identified several pathways to improve our understanding of space457 induced SA, there are limitations to this work. First, due to limited publicly available data 458 on  mice  that  have  been  exposed  to  spaceflight,  the  sample  sizes  are  smaller. 459 Additionally,  our  mouse  and  human  datasets  only  contained  information  from  males, 460 which neglects potential biological outcomes in females. Therefore, sex-based findings 461 from  this  work  should  be  cautioned.  Additionally,  we  only  analyzed  physiological 462 changes in the quadricep muscle and did not investigate the contribution of other limb 463 muscles.  Future studies that incorporate  additional  demographic  and  sex-based 464 information  of  both  males  and  females,  with  the  inclusion  of  muscle  biopsies  and 465 relevant tissue will largely build upon the findings we reported. 466

Likewise, there are limitations to our computational models. One such limitation is in the 468 methodology  of  our  TransComp-R  model.  The  TransComp-R  computational  model 469 selects homologous gene pairs across mouse and human datasets; thus, genes that do 470

Interestingly, GRIN2B was  upregulated in  the skeletal muscle of aging mice. 52 Such consequences would be reflected in how neural populations rhythmically generated activity between flexor and extensor periods. Indeed, when altering synaptic integration properties along the network in our CPG model, primarily synaptic release probability and input gain, we noted a clear reduction in firing coherence between flexor and extensor population, which recapitulates the behavior changes we observed in the gait dynamics.

MATERIALS AND METHODS 493

not  fit  this  criterion  are  omitted.  As  a  result,  there  is  a  possibility  that  these  omitted genes may have also served an active role in the physiological changes to spaceflightinduced SA. Our CPG network model incorporated leaky integrate-and-fire neurons with reciprocal  connections,  synaptic  probabilities,  and  membrane  dynamics.  However, additional models can be incorporated to further explore the mechanistic contribution of protein  altered  during  SA.  For  example,  modeling  a  biophysically  realistic  model  of neurons  with  full  ion  channel  distributions  and  gating  dynamics  could  be  more informative  on  how  changes  at  the  protein  level  affect  neural  dynamics  and  CPG activity.  For  example,  one  can  investigate  how  excessive  synaptic  release  alters integration properties of neurons and their firing rate. Further experiments are needed to causally link the synaptic and protein interactions to behavioral deficits observed during spaceflight or using alternative models of age-related SA.

Our study investigated the  effects  of  SA  as  a  consequence  of spaceflight  travel. We used  the  TransComp-R  approach  and  identified  spaceflight  mouse  PCs  that  could predict  changes  in  human  age-associated  SA  in  muscle  biopsies.  Our  findings  were then validated by matching a panel of genetic markers from these PCs in other human cohorts across different populations. Lastly, our CPG neuron model incorporated with changes in genetic markers of SA was able to recapitulate healthy and space-induced SA  phenotypes  in  locomotion.  Thus,  our  findings  in  this  work  pave  a  path  to  link molecular pathways to behavioral phenotypes of spaced-induced SA.

Data selection 494

We selected  publicly  available  transcriptomics  data  from  the  NASA  OSDR  and  GEO 495 database with the prerequisite that the data contained a balanced number of samples 496 with controls and processed tissue samples from similar quadriceps muscle regions in 497 both mouse and human. 498

499

Our  mouse  dataset  was  selected  from  the  NASA  OSDR.  We  focused  our  selection 500 criteria on transcriptomics data that contained muscle samples near the quadriceps. We 501 identified  a  mouse  study  (OSD-103) that contained a space flight and ground control 502 group. We next searched the GEO database and identified three human SA datasets 503 with  controls  and  biopsy  samples,  each  containing  transcriptomics  data  of  biopsy 504 samples  from  the  vastus  lateralis  muscle.  The  datasets  represent  a  demographic 505 population around the world, including Caucasian (GSE111006), Chinese 506 (GSE111016), and Afro-Caribbean (GSE111010). 507

508

Pre-processing and data normalization 509

We  downloaded  human  transcriptomics  SA  data  from  the  GEO  database  using  the 510 GEO2R  platform  with  Bioconductor  tools  in  R  ( GEOquery ver.  2.70.0, Biobase ver. 511 2.62.0, and limma ver.  3.58.1). We log2 transformed the data and z-score normalized 512 the data per gene. We matched the human and mouse datasets for homologous one-to513 one gene pairing using the orthogene package (ver. 1.8.0). Genes that did not contain 514 homologous pairing were excluded from the model and further downstream analysis. 515

Inference of locomotory activity 635

Population firing  rate  activity  was  temporally  smoothed  with  a  5  ms  Gaussian  kernel. 636 Peak  population  activity  bursts  between  flexor  and  extensor  neurons  were  used  to 637 calculate the phase difference and cycle lengths which were used to infer stride length 638 and  variability,  respectively.  The  correlation  of  variation  was  calculated  by  taking  the 639 mean and standard deviation of cycle lengths between population bursts. To infer swing 640 times, the duration of peak responses in the flexor population was calculated and the 641 differences were taken for each cycle. Similarly, the stance time was taken as the time 642 from the end of the previous swing cycle and the next swing cycle. The ratio of both 643 swing and stance times yielded the swing stance ratio. 644

metadata.preprint_title: An integrative framework linking molecular signatures and locomotory phenotypes in space-induced sarcopenia

pub_metadata.preprint_title: An integrative framework linking molecular signatures and locomotory phenotypes in space-induced sarcopenia
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Ball, B. K.; Khan, H. F.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Age-related skeletal muscle deterioration, referred to as sarcopenia, poses significant risks to astronaut health and mission success during spaceflight, yet its multisystem drivers remain poorly understood. While terrestrial sarcopenia manifests gradually through aging, spaceflight induces analogous musculoskeletal decline within weeks, providing an accelerated model to study conserved atrophy mechanisms. Here, we introduced an integrative framework combining cross-species genetic analysis with physiological modeling to understand mechanistic pathways in space-induced sarcopenia. By analyzing rodent and human datasets, we identified conserved molecular pathways underlying microgravity-induced muscle atrophy, revealing shared regulators of neuromuscular signaling including pathways related to neurotransmitter release and regulation, mitochondrial function, and synaptic integration. Building upon these molecular insights, we developed a physiologically grounded central pattern generator model that reproduced spaceflight-induced locomotion deficits in mice. This multi-scale approach established mechanistic connections between transcriptional changes and impaired movement kinetics while identifying potential therapeutic targets applicable to both spaceflight and terrestrial aging-related muscle loss.
